• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

Processing data for water samples from 20250221 MiSeq run

Processing these data locally for now… 1. Used cutadapt to remove primers 2. unzipped files before going to dada2

library(dada2)
library(dplyr)

# file location
path <- "/Users/diana.baetscher/git-repos/NFS-Bogoslof/MiSeq_data/trimmed"

fnFs <- sort(list.files(path, pattern = "_R1.fastq", full.names = TRUE))
fnRs <- sort(list.files(path, pattern = "_R2.fastq", full.names = TRUE))
# Extract sample names, assuming filenames have format: SAMPLENAME_XXX.fastq
sample.names <- sapply(strsplit(basename(fnFs), "_"), `[`, 1)
plotQualityProfile(fnFs[1:2])
plotQualityProfile(fnRs[1:2])

Read quality… not amazing universally. Probably good to trim somewhat liberally.

# Place filtered files in filtered/ subdirectory
filtFs <- file.path(path, "filtered", paste0(sample.names, "_F_filt.fastq.gz"))
filtRs <- file.path(path, "filtered", paste0(sample.names, "_R_filt.fastq.gz"))
names(filtFs) <- sample.names
names(filtRs) <- sample.names

Initial filtering truncLen failed miserably. We changed it to be super-stringent to see how that affects downstream processing.

out <- filterAndTrim(fnFs, filtFs, fnRs, filtRs, truncLen=c(100,100),
              maxN=0, maxEE=c(2,4), truncQ=2, rm.phix=TRUE,
              compress=TRUE, multithread=TRUE) 
head(out)

Those filters worked decently for now - keeping a majority of the reads.

# ensure there are no samples without reads
# as these don't work in the next steps
out %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  filter(reads.out <1)

# filter the matrix to retain samples with >0 reads

Error rates

# fwd error rates
errF <- learnErrors(filtFs, multithread=TRUE)

# reverse error rates
errR <- learnErrors(filtRs, multithread=TRUE)

# plot the erors
p1 <- plotErrors(errF, nominalQ=TRUE)
p2 <- plotErrors(errR, nominalQ=TRUE)

Sample inference

# forwards
dadaFs <- dada(filtFs, err=errF, multithread=TRUE)

# reverses
dadaRs <- dada(filtRs, err=errR, multithread=TRUE)

Merge paired end reads

mergers <- mergePairs(dadaFs, filtFs, dadaRs, filtRs, verbose=TRUE)
# Inspect the merger data.frame from the first sample
head(mergers[[1]])

Make a sequence table

seqtab <- makeSequenceTable(mergers)
dim(seqtab)
# Inspect distribution of sequence lengths
table(nchar(getSequences(seqtab)))

Let’s remove the singletons and off-target sequences

#seqtab2 <- seqtab[,nchar(colnames(seqtab)) %in% 168:172]
seqtab2 <- seqtab[,nchar(colnames(seqtab)) %in% 162:182]

Remove chimeras

seqtab.nochim <- removeBimeraDenovo(seqtab2, method="consensus", multithread=TRUE, verbose=TRUE)
dim(seqtab.nochim)

Calculate frequency of chimeras

sum(seqtab.nochim)/sum(seqtab2)

Track reads through the pipeline

getN <- function(x) sum(getUniques(x))
track <- cbind(out, sapply(dadaFs, getN), sapply(dadaRs, getN), sapply(mergers, getN), rowSums(seqtab.nochim))

colnames(track) <- c("input", "filtered", "denoisedF", "denoisedR", "merged", "nonchim")
rownames(track) <- sample.names
head(track)

Again, a lot of data loss at the merge step.

Export files for taxonomy and samples/ASVs

 #make fasta file with ASVs
    asv_seqs=colnames(seqtab.nochim)
    for(i in 1:length(asv_seqs))
    {
        write.table(paste(">ASV",i, sep=""),file="csv_outputs/NFS_MiFish_ASV_seqtab_nochim_20250221_wider.csv", append=TRUE, col.names = F, row.names = F, quote=F)
        write.table(paste(asv_seqs[i], sep=""),file="csv_outputs/NFS_MiFish_ASV_seqtab_nochim_20250221_wider.csv", append=TRUE, col.names = F, row.names = F, quote=F)
    }

That’s the input for the FASTA blastn search.

Goal: change ASV headers to numbered ASVs that correspond to those output in the FASTA file.

# Make map between brief names and full sequences
briefToSeq <- colnames(seqtab.nochim)
names(briefToSeq) <- paste0("ASV", seq(ncol(seqtab.nochim))) # Seq1, Seq2, ...
# Make new sequence table with brief names
st.brief <- seqtab.nochim
colnames(st.brief) <- names(briefToSeq)

# export the pool seq table with brief names:
write.csv(st.brief, file="csv_outputs/NFS_MiFish_ASVtable_20250221_wider.csv")

Now move on to the blastn taxonomy search.

Taxonomy outputs

I ran blastn on Sedna and then taxonkit locally.

library(tidyverse)
── Attaching core tidyverse packages ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.4
✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.1
✔ ggplot2   3.5.1     ✔ tibble    3.2.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.3     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1
✔ purrr     1.0.2     ── Conflicts ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the ]8;;http://conflicted.r-lib.org/conflicted package]8;; to force all conflicts to become errors
taxonomy <- read_tsv("../blast_outputs/blastn_taxlineage_NFS_wider.txt", col_names = F) 
Rows: 56269 Columns: 17── Column specification ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: "\t"
chr  (6): X1, X2, X13, X14, X16, X17
dbl (11): X3, X4, X5, X6, X7, X8, X9, X10, X11, X12, X15
ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
head(taxonomy)

tax_df <- taxonomy %>%
  filter(!str_detect(X16, "environmental")) %>% # get rid of any environmental samples 
  select(X1, X3, X17) %>% #remove unnecessary columns
  group_by(X1, X17) %>% # group by the sequence key and the full taxonomy to reduce duplicate entries
  unique() %>% # doing that reduced the number of entries from 146k to 17k
  filter(!is.na(X17)) %>%
  separate(X17, into=c("kingdom", "phylum", "class", "order", "family", "genus", "species"), sep = ";") %>%
  select(-kingdom) %>%
  dplyr::rename(ASV = X1, perc_id = X3) %>%
  filter(!family %in% c("Hominidae", "Bovidae", "Canidae", "Suidae")) # remove these up front.

Taxonomic filtering

Using a 1% identity filter!

For species-level matches: 1. Any ASV that matches only a single species.

tax_asv_top_perc <- tax_df %>%
  mutate(species = ifelse(str_detect(species, " sp\\."), NA, species)) %>% 
  # remove other stuff that isn't on-target or out of range
  filter(!species %in% c("Oncorhynchus kawamurae", "Ammodytes japonicus", "Sebastes baramenuke", "Sebastes cheni", "Sebastes inermis", "uncultured bacterium", "Hexagrammos otakii", "Clupea harengus")) %>% # remove species outside of their range
  filter(family != "Phasianidae") %>%
  group_by(ASV, species) %>%
  slice_max(perc_id, n = 1)
  # take the highest perc_id for a single species (taxonomy) entry per ASV
# this remove entries for a given species/ASV at different percent ids
  
single_spp_taxa <-tax_asv_top_perc %>%
  tally() %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  group_by(ASV) %>%
  tally() %>%
  filter(n == 1) %>%
  left_join(., tax_asv_top_perc, by = "ASV") %>%
  select(-n) %>%
  mutate(taxon = species, taxonomic_level = "species")

61 ASVs have a single species taxonomic match.

# with the multi-species ASVs, rank the species in the top 1% based on identity
id_ranked_df <- tax_asv_top_perc %>%
  anti_join(., single_spp_taxa, by = "ASV") %>%
  group_by(ASV) %>%
  mutate(id_rank = rank(desc(perc_id), ties.method = "min")) %>% # rank the taxonomic hits per sequence by % id
  mutate(top_perc = max(perc_id)) %>% # designate the highest % id for the best taxonomic hit in each sequence (in some, but not all cases, this is 100%) 
  arrange(ASV) %>%
  mutate(perc_diff = top_perc-perc_id) %>%
  filter(!perc_diff > 1) # 1% difference #### EDIT THIS FOR THE % DIFFERENCE ####
  
# now determine which ASVs are single-species based on the top 1%
species_level_asv <- id_ranked_df %>%
  group_by(ASV, species) %>%
  tally() %>%
  group_by(ASV) %>%
  tally() %>%
  filter(n == 1) %>%
  left_join(., id_ranked_df, by = "ASV") %>%
  select(-n) %>%
  filter(perc_id == top_perc) %>%
  filter(!is.na(species)) %>% # remove the sequences without species-level ids
  mutate(taxon = species, taxonomic_level = "species")

an additional 23 ASVs that have a single taxonomic identity within the top 1% of blast matches.

# genus-level
  single_genus <- id_ranked_df %>%
    anti_join(., species_level_asv, by = "ASV") %>% 
    group_by(ASV, genus) %>%
    tally() %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    group_by(ASV) %>%
    tally(name = "n_genera") %>%
    filter(n_genera == 1) %>% # sequences that match only a single genus
    select(-n_genera) %>%
    left_join(., id_ranked_df) %>%
    filter(!is.na(genus)) %>% # get rid of sequences that are NA at the genus level
    mutate(taxonomic_level = "genus") %>%
    mutate(taxon = genus)
Joining with `by = join_by(ASV)`
  
# single family
single_family <- id_ranked_df %>%
    anti_join(., species_level_asv, by = "ASV") %>% 
    anti_join(., single_genus, by = "ASV") %>%
    group_by(ASV, family) %>%
    tally() %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    group_by(ASV) %>%
    tally(name = "n_families") %>%
    filter(n_families == 1) %>% # sequences that match only a single genus
    select(-n_families) %>%
    left_join(., id_ranked_df) %>%
    filter(!is.na(family)) %>% # get rid of sequences that are NA at the genus level
    mutate(taxonomic_level = "family") %>%
    mutate(taxon = family)
Joining with `by = join_by(ASV)`
single_family

# single order
  single_order <- id_ranked_df %>%
    anti_join(., species_level_asv, by = "ASV") %>% 
    anti_join(., single_genus, by = "ASV") %>%
    anti_join(., single_family, by = "ASV") %>%
    group_by(ASV, order) %>%
    tally() %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    group_by(ASV) %>%
    tally() %>%
    filter(n == 1) %>% # sequences that match only a single order
    select(-n) %>%
    left_join(., id_ranked_df) %>%
    filter(!is.na(order)) %>%
    mutate(taxonomic_level = "order") %>%
    mutate(taxon = order)
Joining with `by = join_by(ASV)`
  
  # single class
  single_class <- id_ranked_df %>%
    anti_join(., species_level_asv, by = "ASV") %>% 
    anti_join(., single_genus, by = "ASV") %>%
    anti_join(., single_family, by = "ASV") %>%
    anti_join(single_order, by = "ASV") %>%
    group_by(ASV, class) %>%
    tally() %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    group_by(ASV) %>%
    tally() %>%
    filter(n == 1) %>% # sequences that match only a single class
    select(-n) %>%
    left_join(., id_ranked_df) %>%
    filter(!is.na(class)) %>%
    mutate(taxonomic_level = "class") %>%
    mutate(taxon = class)
Joining with `by = join_by(ASV)`
  
  # single phylum
  single_phylum <- id_ranked_df %>%
   anti_join(., species_level_asv, by = "ASV") %>% 
    anti_join(., single_genus, by = "ASV") %>%
    anti_join(., single_family, by = "ASV") %>%
    anti_join(single_order, by = "ASV") %>%
    anti_join(single_class, by = "ASV") %>%
    group_by(ASV, phylum) %>%
    tally() %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    group_by(ASV) %>%
    tally() %>%
    filter(n == 1) %>% # sequences that match only a single phylum
    select(-n) %>%
    left_join(., id_ranked_df) %>%
    filter(!is.na(phylum)) %>%
    mutate(taxonomic_level = "phylum") %>%
    mutate(taxon = phylum)
Joining with `by = join_by(ASV)`
  
# this dataframe still has multiple taxonomic entries per sequence
# only by selecting the taxon column can we fully collapse the df
taxonomy_combined <- bind_rows(single_spp_taxa, species_level_asv, single_genus, single_family, single_order, single_class, single_phylum) %>%
   mutate(top_perc = ifelse(is.na(top_perc), perc_id, top_perc)) %>%
    mutate(id_rank = ifelse(is.na(id_rank), 1, id_rank)) %>%
     mutate(perc_diff = ifelse(is.na(perc_diff), 0, perc_diff))


# how many species-level assignments are <98% identity?
# let's switch those to genus-level IDs
taxonomy_rank_mult_entries <- taxonomy_combined %>%
  select(-perc_diff) %>%
  #filter(id_rank == 1) %>%  # this will make this df smaller, but the assignment is based on the taxon/taxonomic_level, which includes multiple ranks
  mutate(taxon = ifelse(taxonomic_level == "species" & perc_id < 98, genus, taxon)) %>%
  mutate(taxonomic_level = ifelse(taxonomic_level == "species" & perc_id < 98, "genus", taxonomic_level)) 

taxonomy_rank_mult_entries %>%
  select(taxon, taxonomic_level) %>%
  unique()
NA

330 unique ASV/taxa 36 unique taxa (excluding ASVs)

what non-fish are here?

all of those make sense.

multi_hit_asv_df <- taxonomy_rank_mult_entries %>%
  filter(!is.na(phylum)) %>%
  group_by(ASV) %>%
  add_tally(name = "n_taxonomic_hits") %>%
 # --- STEP 1: extract epithet
  mutate(epithet = word(species, 2)) %>% 

  # --- STEP 2: compute genus- and family-level combined names
  group_by(ASV) %>% 
  mutate(
    # GENUS-LEVEL: "Genus epithet1/epithet2/epithet3"
    combined_genus_epithet = if_else(
      taxonomic_level == "genus" & n_taxonomic_hits < 4,
      paste0(
        first(genus), " ",
        paste(unique(epithet), collapse = "/")
      ),
      taxon
    ),

    # FAMILY-LEVEL: "Genus1/Genus2"
    combined_family_species = if_else(
      taxonomic_level == "family" & n_taxonomic_hits < 4,
      paste(unique(genus), collapse = "/"),
      NA_character_
    )
  ) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  

  # --- STEP 3: choose final hit label
  mutate(
    hit = case_when(
      n_taxonomic_hits == 1 ~ taxon,
      taxonomic_level == "genus"  ~ combined_genus_epithet,
      taxonomic_level == "family" ~ combined_family_species,
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  )
  

samples <- read_csv("csv_outputs/NFS_MiFish_ASVtable_20250221_wider.csv") %>%
  rename(sample = `...1`)
New names:Rows: 369 Columns: 416── Column specification ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr   (1): ...1
dbl (415): ASV1, ASV2, ASV3, ASV4, ASV5, ASV6, ASV7, ASV8, ASV9, ASV10, ASV11, ASV12, ASV13, ASV14, ASV15, ASV16, ASV17, ASV18, A...
ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
multi_tax_sample_df <- samples %>%
  pivot_longer(2:length(samples), names_to = "ASV", values_to = "reads") %>%
  filter(reads > 0) %>%
  left_join(., multi_hit_asv_df) %>%
  filter(!is.na(phylum)) %>%
  mutate(hit = ifelse(is.na(hit), taxon, hit)) %>%
  select(sample, reads, hit) %>%
  unique()
Joining with `by = join_by(ASV)`Warning: Detected an unexpected many-to-many relationship between `x` and `y`.
multi_tax_sample_df
multi_tax_sample_df %>%
  filter(str_detect(sample, "PC")) %>%
   ggplot(aes(x = sample, y = reads, fill = hit)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "stack") +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90)
  ) 

multi_tax_sample_df %>%
  filter(str_detect(sample, "NC")) %>%
   ggplot(aes(x = sample, y = reads, fill = hit)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "stack") +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90)
  )

In the samples that we’re planning on retaining, how many Sturgeon reads?

# filtering steps by read depth, etc.
multi_tax_sample_df %>%
  group_by(sample, hit) %>%
  mutate(spp_reads = sum(reads)) %>%
  group_by(sample) %>%
  mutate(total_reads = sum(spp_reads)) %>%
  filter(total_reads > 1000) %>%
  mutate(prop = spp_reads/total_reads) %>%
    select(sample, hit, spp_reads, total_reads, prop) %>%
    unique() %>%
  separate(sample, into = c("id", "rep"), remove = F) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = sample, y = prop, fill = hit)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "stack") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90)
  ) 
Warning: Expected 2 pieces. Additional pieces discarded in 40 rows [779, 780, 781, 782, 783, 784, 785, 786, 787, 788, 789, 790, 791, 792, 793, 794, 795, 796, 797, 798, ...].

multi_tax_sample_df %>%
  group_by(sample, hit) %>%
  mutate(spp_reads = sum(reads)) %>%
  group_by(sample) %>%
  mutate(total_reads = sum(spp_reads)) %>%
  select(sample, total_reads) %>%
  #filter(total_reads < 20000) %>%
  mutate(keep = ifelse(total_reads > 1500, "keep", "toss")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = log10(total_reads), fill = keep)) +
  geom_histogram()

multi_tax_sample_df %>%
  group_by(sample, hit) %>%
  mutate(spp_reads = sum(reads)) %>%
  group_by(sample) %>%
  mutate(total_reads = sum(spp_reads)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(sample, total_reads) %>%
  unique() %>%
  summarise(median(total_reads))
NA

The number of reads per replicate is wild. I think I probably end up ditching the low-read depth replicates rather than summing them together.

multi_tax_sample_df %>%
  group_by(sample, hit) %>%
  mutate(spp_reads = sum(reads)) %>%
  group_by(sample) %>%
  mutate(total_reads = sum(spp_reads)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(sample, total_reads) %>%
  unique() %>%
  separate(sample, into = c("id", "rep")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = id, y = total_reads, fill = rep)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "stack") +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90)
  ) 
Warning: Expected 2 pieces. Additional pieces discarded in 12 rows [357, 358, 359, 360, 361, 362, 363, 364, 365, 366, 367, 368].

Given that amount of variation, let’s just see what we have using reasonable filters:

168 sample/replicates with >1000 reads

keeper_tax_df <- multi_tax_sample_df %>%
  inner_join(., samples_to_keep) %>%
  group_by(sample, hit) %>%
  mutate(spp_reads = sum(reads)) %>%
  select(sample, hit, spp_reads) %>% # need this here, otherwise the proportions don't sum to 1
  unique() %>%
  group_by(sample) %>%
  mutate(total_reads = sum(spp_reads)) %>%
  filter(total_reads > 1000) %>%
  select(sample, hit, spp_reads, total_reads) %>%
  unique() %>%
  mutate(prop = spp_reads/total_reads)
keeper_df %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = sample, y = prop, fill = hit)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "stack") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0,0))

ggsave("pdf_outputs/eDNA_quicklook_20260120.png", height = 10, width = 10)

Next steps: 1. Sum across replicates for a given sample 2. Add in metadata

slim_df <- keeper_df %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  separate(sample, into = c("extraction_ID", "rep"), remove = F) %>%
  select(-sample, -rep, -spp_reads) %>%
  rename(taxa = hit) %>% 
  # take the mean proportion of reads by taxon across replicates
  group_by(extraction_ID, taxa) %>%
  summarise(mean_prop = mean(prop))
slim_df %>%
  filter(sum(mean_prop) > 1) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = extraction_ID, y = mean_prop, fill = taxa)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "stack") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0,0))

NA
NA

combined_meta_w_edna <- meta %>%
  left_join(., slim_df) %>%
  rename(proportion_sequencing_reads = new_prop) %>%
  select(-taxa_prop, -new_tot_prop)
Joining with `by = join_by(extraction_ID)`
combined_meta_w_edna %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = extraction_ID, y = proportion_sequencing_reads, fill = taxa)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "stack") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0,0))

ggsave("pdf_outputs/eDNA_by_filter.png", height = 10, width = 15)

the other way to provide these data to MML is by station, i.e., summing across the three filters at a given station.

combined_meta_w_edna %>%
  write_csv("csv_outputs/Bogoslof_eDNA_w_metadata_20260121.csv")

# single list per sampling location:
combined_meta_w_edna %>%
  select(-proportion_sequencing_reads) %>%
  group_by(Transect, StationKM) %>%
  mutate()
